points: Parity refers to whether a number is even (arrangeable in pairs) or odd (one leftover); key rules: even ± even = even, odd ± odd = even, and even ± odd = odd — these allow us to determine the possibility of a result without computation.

The sum of any number of even numbers is always even; the sum of an odd count of odd numbers is always odd; the sum of an even count of odd numbers is always even — so choosing 5 odd cards to sum to an even number like 30 is impossible.
The product m × n is odd only when both m and n are odd; if at least one is even, the product is even — this determines parity of grid cells without multiplying.

In a 3×3 grid filled with numbers 1–9 (no repetition), all row sums and all column sums each total 45; for a magic square (where every row, column, and diagonal are equal), the magic sum must be 45 ÷ 3 = 15, and the centre cell must be 5.

The numbers 1 and 9 cannot appear in corner positions of a 3×3 magic square — they must occupy edge (non-corner) positions; the generalised structure with centre m has cells expressed as m±1, m±2, m±3, m±4, giving magic sum = 3m.

Any uniform operation on a magic square preserves the magic property: adding a constant k to all cells increases the magic sum by 3k; multiplying all cells by k multiplies the magic sum by k — enabling magic squares for any desired set of consecutive numbers.

The Virahāṅka sequence 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34, … was first discovered in the study of poetic rhythms; each term equals the sum of the two preceding terms (V(n) = V(n−1) + V(n−2)), and the nth Virahāṅka number equals the number of ways to write n as a sum of 1s and 2s.

The parity pattern of the Virahāṅka sequence repeats every 3 positions: O, E, O, O, E, O, … — a term at position n is even if n mod 3 = 2, otherwise odd; the nth even number = 2n and the nth odd number = 2n − 1.

Cryptarithms (alphametics) are arithmetic puzzles where each digit is replaced by a letter; they are solved systematically from the units column upward — for example, T + T + T = UT gives T = 5, U = 1, since 3 × 5 = 15.
